question: A patient underwent intracranial stent placement. Attempts at balloon angioplasty to optimize the device position were unsuccessful and the case was managed conservatively. Within one hour the patient had acute neurological deterioration and repeat angiography was performed. Based on the image, which complication most likely explains the clinical deterioration?
ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the image carefully. This is a 3D angiographic view of the intracranial circulation showing a deployed endovascular device within a curved arterial segment. There is a focal area marked by an asterisk where the stent appears incompletely opposed to the vessel wall at an angulated bend.

Next, I relate these imaging findings to the clinical question. The stem states balloon angioplasty to optimize stent position was unsuccessful and the patient acutely deteriorated within one hour, prompting repeat angiography. The key imaging correlate to explain an immediate post-procedure neurologic decline should be something that can occur rapidly at a site of incomplete stent apposition.

Then, I consider how each choice aligns with the image and timing. Intracranial hemorrhage would typically show contrast extravasation or would be suggested clinically/radiographically as parenchymal blood; there is no angiographic evidence of frank extravasation on this study. Arterial dissection would often produce a visible flap or double lumen or abrupt focal narrowing distinct from the device, which is not apparent here. Device migration causing a new occlusion would require the stent to be displaced from the treated segment; the device is still seen at the curved segment rather than displaced. Distal thromboembolism would produce abrupt distal cutoff(s) downstream, but the principal abnormality localized to the stented, malapposed segment makes a local intraluminal process more likely.

Finally, the combination of a malapposed intracranial stent at an acute time point after deployment and rapid neurologic decline is most consistent with formation of thrombus within the stent lumen (acute in‑stent thrombosis), which can rapidly occlude flow and cause acute ischemia. Therefore acute in‑stent thrombosis best explains the clinical deterioration shown.
caption: Left unruptured M1 dissecting/saccular aneurysm. (A) A 3.5×25 mm device was deployed from M1 to the supraclinoid ICA. The proximal landing zone was located into an arterial angulation (*), and the device did not appose fully to the ICA wall. After unsuccessful attempts of repositioning with balloon angioplasty, it was managed conservatively. (B) One hour after the procedure, the patient developed right hemiparesis and a DSA showed stent thrombosis (arrow) secondary to the proximal stent malapposition. (C) A bolus of tirofiban was administered and a Leo+ stent was proximally telescoped allowing a fully wall apposition. (D) Both stents remained opened, fully patent with no flow compromise. DSA, digital subtraction angiography; ICA, internal carotid artery.
sub_caption: (A) A 3.5×25 mm device was deployed from M1 to the supraclinoid ICA. The proximal landing zone was located into an arterial angulation (*), and the device did not appose fully to the ICA wall. After unsuccessful attempts of repositioning with balloon angioplasty, it was managed conservatively.
image_context: In both cases of malapposition, secondary vessel angulation and stent positioning balloon angioplasty failed to resolve the malapposition. One case was initially managed conservatively but thrombosed 1 hour post-procedure despite adequate antiplatelet medication and resulted in acute neurological deterioration. The patient underwent a repeat procedure with implantation of a Leo+ stent (Balt, Montmorency, France) proximally to fully open the implanted SV, improving the clinical condition with no neurological sequelae (table 3, <xref rid="neurintsurg-2021-017421f03" ref-type="fig">figure 3</xref>). In the second case, an Atlas stent (Stryker Neurovascular, Fremont, CA) was telescoped distally with no clinical sequelae.). In the second case, an Atlas stent (Stryker Neurovascular, Fremont, CA) was telescoped distally with no clinical sequelae. Four (7%) events were documented post-procedure as follows: an immediate post-procedural stent thrombosis (table 3, <xref rid="neurintsurg-2021-017421f03" ref-type="fig">figure 3</xref>) resolved with a telescoped Leo+ stent (no clinical consequences but an ischemic stroke in the left basal ganglia on 24 hours CT was reported); one external ventricular drain tract hemorrhage post-procedure in a case of sub-arachnoid hemorrhage (SAH) (stable after eptifibatide shut off, resolved at time of discharge from hospital with no new focal neurological deficits); one hemi-pontine stroke in a case of symptomatic dissecting mid-basilar aneurysm; and one groin superficial hematoma with no associated pseudoaneurysm.) resolved with a telescoped Leo+ stent (no clinical consequences but an ischemic stroke in the left basal ganglia on 24 hours CT was reported); one external ventricular drain tract hemorrhage post-procedure in a case of sub-arachnoid hemorrhage (SAH) (stable after eptifibatide shut off, resolved at time of discharge from hospital with no new focal neurological deficits); one hemi-pontine stroke in a case of symptomatic dissecting mid-basilar aneurysm; and one groin superficial hematoma with no associated pseudoaneurysm.

One case was initially managed conservatively but thrombosed 1 hour post-procedure despite adequate antiplatelet medication and resulted in acute neurological deterioration. The patient underwent a repeat procedure with implantation of a Leo+ stent (Balt, Montmorency, France) proximally to fully open the implanted SV, improving the clinical condition with no neurological sequelae (table 3, figure 3). In the second case, an Atlas stent (Stryker Neurovascular, Fremont, CA) was telescoped distally with no clinical sequelae.). In the second case, an Atlas stent (Stryker Neurovascular, Fremont, CA) was telescoped distally with no clinical sequelae. Four (7%) events were documented post-procedure as follows: an immediate post-procedural stent thrombosis (table 3, figure 3) resolved with a telescoped Leo+ stent (no clinical consequences but an ischemic stroke in the left basal ganglia on 24 hours CT was reported); one external ventricular drain tract hemorrhage post-procedure in a case of sub-arachnoid hemorrhage (SAH) (stable after eptifibatide shut off, resolved at time of discharge from hospital with no new focal neurological deficits); one hemi-pontine stroke in a case of symptomatic dissecting mid-basilar aneurysm; and one groin superficial hematoma with no associated pseudoaneurysm.) resolved with a telescoped Leo+ stent (no clinical consequences but an ischemic stroke in the left basal ganglia on 24 hours CT was reported); one external ventricular drain tract hemorrhage post-procedure in a case of sub-arachnoid hemorrhage (SAH) (stable after eptifibatide shut off, resolved at time of discharge from hospital with no new focal neurological deficits); one hemi-pontine stroke in a case of symptomatic dissecting mid-basilar aneurysm; and one groin superficial hematoma with no associated pseudoaneurysm. reasoning.claim_text: Given the malapposed stent seen in the curved intracranial segment and the knowledge that thrombus within a stent can rapidly occlude flow and cause acute ischemia, formation of thrombus within the stent lumen is a consistent explanation for the immediate post-procedure neurologic decline.
